story:
At sunrise, Little Billy—the smallest brother with the biggest hunger—decides the stubby, juicy grass across Meadow Bridge is worth the risk. Trip, trap, trip, trap go his hooves, until a shadow rises: two green ears, a neon safety vest, and a clipboard. The so-called Monster introduces himself (sort of) by rumbling about “community-shared footpaths requiring gentle crossing to reduce impact loads and acoustic stress.” He’s Tormund River-Overbridge, Leader of Layouts for Structural Integrity and Stream Ecology—T.R.O.L.L., laminated badge and all. He’s not into gobbling; he’s into wrenches, ballast rocks, and therapy flashcards: Use “I” statements. Big feelings don’t make you big and scary. Boundaries are not bridges, but they keep bridges from breaking. Also, loud hooves spike his misophonia. Little hears “impact” and translates it to danger, bleats, “Please don’t eat me! Wait for my bigger brother—he’s crunchier!” and bolts.

Middle Billy, armed with skepticism and opinions, declares trolls a myth and strides onto the bridge with a cadence like taiko drums. Tormund tries his “assertive voice,” which sounds like a thundercloud gargling gravel, and even attempts clarity: “I am not going to gobble you”—but the goat only hears gobble. Tormund silently pleads his case: he’s plant-based, goat cheese upsets his ethics, and he’s been mocked and disowned for it. Middle freezes, panics, nominates Big as a “banquet,” and scrambles off.

Enter Big Billy, bearded, broad, and allergic to bullies. He stomps; the timbers groan; the river hisses like a kettle. Tormund, shaking behind a clipboard and hard hat, warns: the bridge is rated for one large hoofed ruminant at a time, and there’s an endangered trout nursery under Span B. As Big lowers his horns, Tormund is hit with a flashback—kids yelling “monster,” rocks thrown, the learned reflex to be loud before he can be kind. He remembers his therapist: Name the feeling, not the person. “I’m not mad,” he blurts. “I’m anxious. I care about this bridge—goats, trouts, and mornings and possibilities. Every trip, every trap sounds like a countdown to a crumble.”

Big pauses and sees a heart instead of a threat. He softens his hooves. “You could’ve just said, ‘Go one at a time.’” Tormund shows his flashcards, including “Try a compliment sandwich.” They practice: nice vest, cracked plank, excellent lamination.

Then a crack pings through Span B. Tormund lunges—not for a goat, but to wedge a beam and his own back beneath the weakest board. “Everyone off the center—feather steps—one at a time!” Big shepherds his brothers like ballerinos in a neon tutu of virtue. The bridge holds. On the far side, the meadow opens like a green curtain.

Little returns with a wildflower and thanks Tormund for saving their hooves—and the trout. Tormund apologizes for his avalanche voice and posts the sign he’d been too shy to share: Bridge Etiquette: one goat at a time; soft steps on the center span; compliments encouraged; say hello to the Leader (Moro reflex issues). Big taps it in; Middle straightens it; Little draws a smiling trout.

They admit they jumped to gobbling conclusions. They picnic—beet hummus and riparian buffer facts—and learn a bridge is a promise: I’ll hold you up if you treat me kindly. Hooves become music when you trust the musicians. Travelers are introduced to “Tormund, the Leader. He’s not scary; he’s careful.” A second sign appears: MONSTERS WELCOME (Especially the Misunderstood Kind). Tormund sheds exactly one legal tear. When a child asks, “Are you a bad guy?” he answers, “Sometimes people see teeth and forget teeth are also for smiling. I get loud when I’m scared. I want things safe and fair.” The kid gives him a star sticker; it feels like a promotion.

If you cross Meadow Bridge and hear a rumble, don’t run. It’s a reminder about cadence and courtesy. From the right angle, that mossy moon of a face is smiling.

One goat at a time. Soft steps. Compliment sandwiches. And room on the bridge for every kind of traveler.
